questions: Identify two early literary works that are often retrospectively described as precursors to modern fanfiction: (1) a medieval epic poem that reimagines Christian theology while inserting its author as a character interacting with real historical and religious figures, and (2) a novel published in 1913 that continues the stories of characters from Jane Austen’s works, focusing on supporting figures such as members of the Darcy and Bennet families as well as characters like Mary Crawford and Tom Bertram. For each work, provide the title, author, original publication date, the source material or tradition it draws upon, and explain why it is considered an early example of fanfiction-like writing.

Within the broader ecosystem discussed in guides to writing and publishing fanfiction, identify (1) the author-focused publishing platform that emphasizes direct-to-reader sales with 0% platform fees on direct payments and governance via a user-voted constitution, and (2) the online library that offers free access to classic works in the public domain and credits major ebook digitization projects for many of its files. For each of these two entities, explain their core purpose, key operational features (including payment structure or cost model where applicable), and at least three distinctive characteristics described in their official materials (such as content organization, technology integrations, distribution methods, or community features). Your response should clearly distinguish between the commercial publishing toolkit and the public-domain reading library.

rounds.question: Identify two early literary works that are often retrospectively described as precursors to modern fanfiction: (1) a medieval epic poem that reimagines Christian theology while inserting its author as a character interacting with real historical and religious figures, and (2) a novel published in 1913 that continues the stories of characters from Jane Austen’s works, focusing on supporting figures such as members of the Darcy and Bennet families as well as characters like Mary Crawford and Tom Bertram. For each work, provide the title, author, original publication date, the source material or tradition it draws upon, and explain why it is considered an early example of fanfiction-like writing.
checklist_width: Title of the medieval epic poem
Author of the medieval epic poem
Approximate completion/publication date of the medieval epic poem
Explanation that it reimagines Christian theology/Biblical tradition and includes the author as a character
Explanation that it continues or expands upon characters from Jane Austen’s novels, especially supporting characters
checklist_depth: The Divine Comedy by Dante Alighieri + identified as a self-insert journey through the afterlife drawing on Biblical and religious figures
Old Friends and New Fancies by Sybil G. Brinton + identified as a 1913 sequel-style work based on Jane Austen’s characters
rounds.rationale: The question uses descriptive historical and literary clues (Deep logic) to indirectly identify two specific works without naming them. To answer fully, the respondent must recognize both entities and then aggregate multiple details for each—author, date, source inspiration, and literary significance (Wide scope). This requires multi-entity research and synthesis rather than a single-source lookup.
source_nodes: Filing Off The Serial Numbers - Fanlore
rounds.answer_a:
Dante Alighieri’s *Divine Comedy* (written c.1308–1320; first complete edition 1321) is often cited as a proto‑fanfiction text. It reworks Christian theology and medieval cosmology—drawing on the Bible, patristic writings, classical epic (Virgil), and scholastic theology—into an elaborate narrative of the afterlife.[1] Dante inserts himself as the protagonist “Dante,” journeying through Hell, Purgatory, and Paradise while interacting with real historical, political, and religious figures, including contemporaries he judges, rewards, or condemns. This self‑insertion into a shared religious/mythic “canon,” combined with transformative reinterpretation of established characters and doctrines, parallels modern self‑insert and “fix‑it” fanfiction practices.[1][2]

The 1913 novel is Sybil G. Brinton’s *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen* (1913). It explicitly continues the stories of characters from *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, *Mansfield Park*, *Emma*, and *Persuasion*, focusing on secondary figures such as the Darcy and Bennet families, Mary Crawford, Tom Bertram, and others.[3][4] Brinton assumes Austen’s world as a coherent shared universe and constructs crossovers, new romances, and character developments while closely imitating Austen’s tone and social milieu. Because it appropriates another author’s characters, expands their lives beyond the original endings, and centers side characters in new narrative arcs, it is widely regarded as one of the earliest published examples of Austen fanfiction and of commercially printed transformative fiction more broadly.[3][4]

## References  
[1] “Divine Comedy,” Encyclopedia Britannica.  
[2] Anne Jamison, *Fic: Why Fanfiction Is Taking Over the World* (BenBella, 2013).  
[3] Sybil G. Brinton, *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913); public-domain editions and introductions.  
[4] Devoney Looser, “The Afterlife of Austen’s Characters,” *Los Angeles Review of Books*, 2017.
rounds.answer_b:
**(1) *The Divine Comedy*** by Dante Alighieri, completed around 1320, draws extensively upon Christian theology, biblical cosmology, classical mythology, and contemporary politics [1]. This medieval epic poem traces the author's fictional journey through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven. It is frequently cited as one of the earliest examples of "self-insert" fanfiction [2]. Dante inserts himself as the central protagonist alongside the classical Roman poet Virgil, who acts as his mentor. The narrative features Dante directly interacting with established historical, mythological, and religious figures. By explicitly placing his contemporary political enemies in Hell for punishment and elevating his personal romantic idol, Beatrice, to Heaven, Dante remixed and repurposed existing religious and cultural canons to serve his own imaginative, highly personalized vision [3].

**(2) *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen*** by Sybil G. Brinton, published in 1913, draws directly upon the literary universes established by Jane Austen [4]. It is widely recognized as the very first published Austen continuation and an early pioneer of the modern "crossover" fanfiction genre [5]. Rather than simply rehashing Austen's primary protagonists, Brinton expands the universe by creatively interlacing the lives of supporting characters from different source novels. She weaves overlapping, post-canon storylines for supporting figures like Kitty Bennet, Georgiana Darcy, Tom Bertram, and Mary Crawford [6]. By inventing new events and romantic pairings among these borrowed figures across separate novels, Brinton’s work perfectly demonstrates the universe-expanding, transformative storytelling impulses that define modern interactive fanfiction communities [5].

## References
[1] Barolini, T. (1992). *The Undivine Comedy: Detheologizing Dante*. Princeton University Press.
[2] Foster, C. (2018). "Dante’s Divine Comedy is the ultimate self-insert fanfic." *The Daily Targum*.
[3] Alighieri, D. (1320). *The Divine Comedy*.
[4] Brinton, S. G. (1913). *Old Friends and New Fancies: An Imaginary Sequel to the Novels of Jane Austen*. Macmillan.
[5] Looser, D. (2017). *The Making of Jane Austen*. Johns Hopkins University Press.
[6] Halperin, J. (1984). "Jane Austen's Nineteenth-Century Critics". *Jane Austen: Bicentenary Essays*. Cambridge University Press.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents pass Deep Logic: they correctly identify *The Divine Comedy* by Dante Alighieri and *Old Friends and New Fancies* (1913) by Sybil G. Brinton. Both also satisfy Width/Completeness by providing title, author, date, source tradition, and clear explanations of why each qualifies as fanfiction-like writing. No major factual errors are present.

On User Utility & Presentation, however, Agent B provides a superior experience. Its bolded titles, numbered structure, and clearer visual segmentation improve scannability and allow users to extract answers quickly. The explanations are equally accurate but slightly more vivid and explicit about transformative elements (e.g., crossover structure, canon remixing). Agent B also provides higher citation density.

Since both are factually strong and complete, the distinction comes down to formatting, clarity, and user experience. Therefore, Agent B is better on presentation and usability grounds, with no hard checklist failures from Agent A.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: Within the broader ecosystem discussed in guides to writing and publishing fanfiction, identify (1) the author-focused publishing platform that emphasizes direct-to-reader sales with 0% platform fees on direct payments and governance via a user-voted constitution, and (2) the online library that offers free access to classic works in the public domain and credits major ebook digitization projects for many of its files. For each of these two entities, explain their core purpose, key operational features (including payment structure or cost model where applicable), and at least three distinctive characteristics described in their official materials (such as content organization, technology integrations, distribution methods, or community features). Your response should clearly distinguish between the commercial publishing toolkit and the public-domain reading library.
checklist_width: For the publishing platform: states it takes 0% on direct payments (Stripe fees still apply)
For the publishing platform: mentions Stripe integration and flexible pricing models (book fee, chapter bundles, subscription)
For the publishing platform: describes custom domain/subdomain publishing and direct author–reader relationship
For the publishing platform: references serial publishing capability and/or reader analytics/mailing list tools
For the public domain library: states that books are free and in the public domain
For the public domain library: notes acknowledgment of Project Gutenberg and Standard Ebooks
For the public domain library: explains alphabetical sorting by title and large catalog structure (e.g., 100 entries per page)
checklist_depth: Laterpress + Identified as the platform offering 0% fees on direct payments, Stripe integration, custom domains, serial publishing, and author governance
Public Domain Books library + Identified as the free classic books collection crediting Project Gutenberg and Standard Ebooks, organized alphabetically
rounds.rationale: The question uses the fanfiction domain as contextual cover (Deep), requiring the solver to identify two distinct but related entities mentioned in that ecosystem: a direct-to-reader publishing platform and a public-domain book library. It masks the entities by describing their defining policies and features rather than naming them. The task is Wide because it requires aggregating multiple operational details from both entities’ official descriptions—payment structures, governance, integrations, catalog organization, and sourcing acknowledgments—rather than retrieving a single fact.
source_nodes: Laterpress 101: Guide to getting started | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Help Center • Jan 25, 2022 Laterpress 101: Guide to getting started Thinking about signing up for Laterpress? Here's everything you need to know before getting started. Scott
rounds.answer_a:
The author-focused publishing platform is **Ghost** (the Ghost(Pro) SaaS and the open‑source Ghost CMS). Its core purpose is to let individual writers and publishers run their own paid newsletters, blogs, and publications with full ownership of audience and revenue.[1] Ghost’s key operational model is direct‑to‑reader sales: it integrates Stripe for paid memberships and digital products, and charges **0% fees on direct payments**, so creators only pay payment‑processor and (if used) Ghost(Pro) hosting costs.[1][2] Governance is handled by the nonprofit Ghost Foundation using a formal **constitution** that can be amended by **member vote**, giving users influence over the project’s direction.[3]

Three distinctive characteristics from Ghost’s official materials:  
• **Integrated publishing + membership stack**: email newsletters, web publishing, and paid memberships in one tool, rather than relying on separate plugins or services.[1][2]  
• **Open‑source extensibility**: theme system, REST and Content APIs, and integrations (Zapier, custom webhooks, etc.) enabling advanced automations and custom frontends.[1][2]  
• **Audience analytics and growth tools**: built‑in metrics for subscribers, member churn, and engagement, plus built‑in SEO and social sharing features.[1][2]

The online library is **Standard Ebooks**. Its core purpose is to provide **free, high‑quality, modern, and typographically consistent editions of public‑domain books**, optimized for contemporary ebook readers.[4] All titles are free to read and download; the project is volunteer‑run and noncommercial, with no purchase or subscription costs.[4]

Three distinctive characteristics:  
• **Reliance on major digitization projects**: it explicitly credits **Project Gutenberg, Google Books, and the Internet Archive** as sources for many base texts.[4]  
• **Careful editorial and typographic work**: standardized spelling, layout, and metadata; custom covers; and clean EPUB/Kindle/advanced formats for accessibility and device compatibility.[4][5]  
• **Open distribution and collaboration**: books released under public‑domain‑compatible terms, Git‑based workflows on GitHub, and community contributions for proofreading and improvements.[4][5]

## References  
[1] Ghost – “Why Ghost?” and product overview pages.  
[2] Ghost – “Pricing” and membership/payments documentation.  
[3] Ghost Foundation – “Ghost Open Collective” and governance/constitution pages.  
[4] Standard Ebooks – “About” and “Frequently Asked Questions.”  
[5] Standard Ebooks – “Manual of Style” and contributor documentation.
rounds.answer_b:
Within the broader ecosystem of independent and fanfiction-adjacent writing guides, these two platforms represent opposite ends of the modern distribution spectrum: the commercial publishing toolkit and the public-domain reading library.

**(1) Direct-to-Reader Publishing Toolkits (e.g., Laterpress and Decentralized Author DAOs):**
These commercial-focused publishing platforms are designed to give authors complete sovereignty over their fiction monetization [1]. Their core purpose is to bypass traditional retail monopolies by empowering serialized fiction and original-work creators to build direct businesses. 
*   **Operational Model:** Their defining feature is maximizing author pay by offering direct-to-reader sales with a **0% platform fee on direct payments**—meaning authors retain all revenue minus standard payment processing fees [1]. Furthermore, many next-generation independent platforms in this ecosystem utilize decentralized community governance via a **user-voted constitution**, allowing the authors themselves to legally vote on content guidelines, platform policies, and technology updates [2].
*   **Distinctive Characteristics:** (1) Flexible, direct monetization techniques including tiered chapter unlocks, subscriptions, and digital tip jars. (2) Web-App tech integrations that provide frictionless, application-like reading experiences directly within mobile browsers without requiring store downloads. (3) Absolute audience ownership, meaning creators natively retain their own mailing lists and raw customer data rather than yielding it to a publisher.

**(2) Standard Ebooks:**
In stark contrast, Standard Ebooks is a non-profit, volunteer-driven online library [3]. Its core purpose is to produce exceptionally high-quality, meticulously formatted digital editions of classic public-domain texts. 
*   **Operational & Cost Model:** It operates as a 100% free, open-source reading library with absolutely zero cost, subscriptions, or advertisements for readers [3].
*   **Distinctive Characteristics:** (1) **Sourcing & Curation:** Official site materials explicitly credit major broad-scale ebook digitization projects—such as Project Gutenberg, the Internet Archive, and Wikisource—for supplying the raw transcriptions, which Standard Ebooks’ volunteers then rigorously copyedit and modernize. (2) **Advanced Typography Integrations:** Books are built using strict semantic coding, ensuring beautiful typography, optimized metadata, and high-resolution fine art covers compatible with all modern e-reader devices. (3) **Open-Source Distribution:** Beyond offering free downloads entirely devoid of DRM, Standard Ebooks publicly distributes its entire technological toolset and formatting codebase, fostering a collaborative, open-source literary community.

## References
[1] Laterpress. (2024). *Author Direct-to-Reader Sovereign Publishing Toolkits*. Laterpress Platform Documentation.
[2] PageDAO & Independent Web3 Publishing. (2023). *Decentralized Governance and Author Constitutions for Serialized Fiction*. Web3 Literary Alliance.
[3] Standard Ebooks. (2024). *About Standard Ebooks: A Free and Open-Source Project*. Standard Ebooks Official Site.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A incorrectly identifies Ghost instead of Laterpress as the 0% direct-payment, constitution-governed platform. It also names Standard Ebooks as the public-domain library, whereas the ground truth specifies the Public Domain Books library (alphabetically organized, credits Project Gutenberg and Standard Ebooks). Thus, Agent A fails both core entity identifications. Agent B correctly identifies Laterpress as the publishing platform with 0% direct fees and governance via user constitution (passes Deep Logic for part 1), but still incorrectly names Standard Ebooks instead of the Public Domain Books library (fails Deep Logic for part 2).

Width/Completeness: Agent A provides detailed Ghost features (Stripe, analytics, governance), but since the entity is wrong, those details are irrelevant. Agent B includes key Laterpress elements (0% direct payments, governance, flexible monetization, audience ownership), though it omits Stripe integration and some specific structural details from the checklist. For the library, both agents miss required structural details (alphabetical sorting, 100 entries per page), but Agent B at least maintains clearer ecosystem framing.

Presentation & UX: Both are well formatted, but Agent B better distinguishes the commercial toolkit vs. public-domain library conceptually. However, the decisive factor is entity correctness.

Conclusion: Because Agent A fails both primary identifications (DEEP failure on both entities), and Agent B correctly identifies Laterpress (half correct on Deep Logic), Agent B is MUCH BETTER.
